hints:
Comparative formula: **adjective/adverb + -er/more + than** (hot → hotter than; dry → drier than).
Equality formula: **not (the) same + as** and **not as + adjective + as** for negating equality.
Superlative formula: **the + most/least + adjective** for the highest or lowest degree of quality.

a1.hotter: Comparative is formed according to the scheme base adjective + -er + than, so hot → hotter + than.
a1.hottest: Superlative requires the formula the + adjective-est or the most + adjective, not a comparison with than.
a1.hoter: When forming hotter, we double the final consonant before -er: hot → hotter.

a2.than: After comparative, the conjunction than is used: comparative + than + object.
a2.that: The conjunction that is not included in the formula comparative + than.
a2.as: As is used in the structure as + adjective + as for equal comparisons, not after comparative.

a3.us: After than in comparisons, we put the objective case (me/us/them).
a3.our: The possessive pronoun does not correspond to the formula than + object pronoun.
a3.we: The subject pronoun without an auxiliary verb does not fit in the scheme comparative + than + object.

a4.as: Equality comparisons are formatted through the structure (not) the same as / as + adjective + as.
a4.that: Same + that does not form a standard similarity construction.
a4.than: Than is included in the formula comparative + than, not the same as.

a5.least: The superlative of the lower degree has the structure the + least + adjective.
a5.more: More creates a comparative (more + adjective + than) that does not convey the highest/lowest degree.
a5.less: Less is a regular comparative without the; for the lowest degree, the formula the least + adjective is needed.

a6.ever: The collocation the + superlative + ever emphasizes the best/worst experience in life.
a6.never: Never changes the meaning of the construction ever + present perfect, which describes experience.
a6.before: Before without ever does not provide the formula for superlative experience (the best ... ever).

a7.nice as: The structure not as + adjective + as uses the base form of the adjective between two as.
a7.nicer as: Comparative with as violates the formula; after comparative comes than.
a7.nicer than: The formula not as ... as does not require comparative, only the base form of the adjective.
a7.as nice as: The full model as + adjective + as shows equality when both elements as are present.

a8.lot more: A common conversational model is a lot more + adjective; without the article, it violates the formula.
a8.most: Most + adjective forms the superlative (the most ...), not a comparison with than/as.
a8.far more: Another intensifier in the scheme far + more + adjective.

a9.in: The phrase one of the + superlative + plural noun + in the world uses the preposition in.
a9.of: The preposition of in this position does not fit the standard formula one of the most + noun + in the world.
a9.from: From denotes origin and does not combine with the superlative framework one of the most ... in the world.

a10.as much: The structure of quantity for uncountable: as much + uncountable noun + as.
a10.as many: The formula as many + countable noun + as applies only to countable nouns.
a10.more: Comparative of quantity more + noun + than does not correspond to the model as much ... as.

hints: The formula for comparing two items: comparative (more + adjective) + than + object.

a1.more bitter than: Multisyllabic adjectives form comparative according to the scheme more + adjective + than.
a1.bitterest than: The superlative adjective-est is used with the and without than.
a1.more bitter that: The conjunction in the comparative formula is than, not that.
a1.far more bitter than: The intensifier far can stand before more: far + more + adjective + than.

hints: Rule: one-syllable and some two-syllable adjectives → base adjective + -er + than.

a1.happier: Words ending in -y change -y to -ier in the comparative form: happy → happier.
a1.more happy: For short adjectives, the suffix -er is used instead of the construction more + adjective.
a1.more happier: Double comparative (more + -er) contradicts the grading formula.
a1.far happier: The intensifier far is added before the ready comparative: far + comparative + than.

hints: The formula for adverbs ending in -ly: more/less + adverb + than for comparing speed or intensity.

a1.more slowly: Adverbs form the comparative through more + adverb: more slowly + than ...
a1.slowlier: Adding -er to adverbs ending in -ly does not follow the grading rule.
a1.most slowly: Most + adverb forms the superlative, not the comparative form.
a1.a bit more slowly: Intensifiers (a bit, slightly) can be placed before more + adverb.

hints:
Template for lack of difference: any/no + comparative + than, without the construction as + comparative.
Sentences with two correct options reflect the same meaning through different intensifiers (any/no).

The model any + comparative + than indicates that there is actually no difference.
No + comparative + than means equality in the feature (the price is the same).
As ... as requires the base form of the adjective: as + adjective + as.

hints: Structure of gradual change: comparative and comparative (worse and worse) to denote a trend.

a1.worse and worse: The fixed pattern comparative and comparative emphasizes gradual deterioration.
a1.more and more worse: The combination more + worse violates the rule: irregular adjective bad → worse (without more).
a1.every day badder: Bad has the irregular comparative worse; the form badder is not used.
a1.steadily worse: Another way to convey the trend: steadily + comparative.

hints: The negative construction not as ... as has the same meaning as the comparison with than in reverse order.

Direct form comparative: subject + verb + more + adjective + than + object.
The sentence mirrors the logic of comparison and makes David smarter.
Not as + adjective + as conveys that the subject has a lower degree of the feature than the object.

hints: Template as + adjective/adverb + as for maximum effort or equality.

a1.as fast as: Standard template as + adverb + as for maximum speed.
a1.as faster as: The construction as ... as does not contain comparative inside; the base form is needed.
a1.faster as: The order of elements is violated: the formula requires two as on the edges and the base form between them.
a1.as quickly as: The synonymous variant with quickly corresponds to the same formula as + adverb + as.

hints: After than, you can place the object pronoun (me) or a shortened repetition of the verb (I do).

a1.me: In the formula comparative + than + object pronoun, the object pronoun is used after than.
a1.I do: A short answer after than can repeat the auxiliary verb: than I do.
a1.I have: The repetition of the verb have creates a heavy structure and does not fit into the usual pattern than + auxiliary.

hints: Ever + present perfect emphasizes experience; superlative: the most + adjective.

a1.the most boring teacher I've ever met: The superlative according to the formula the + most + adjective with the specification of experience ever + present perfect.
a1.the most boring teacher I've never met: Never met contradicts the application about the meeting: the construction denies the very fact of experience.
a1.The boringest teacher I've never met: The form boringest is stylistically marginal, and never met removes the experience.
a1.the very boringest teacher: Without the specification of experience (ever met) and with the simplified boringest, the expression looks unfixed.

hints: Fixed collocation: the best/worst day **of** my life.

a1.of: The collocation the + superlative + noun + of my life uses the preposition of.
a1.in: In my life is possible, but in the fixed expression the best day of my life, traditionally, of is placed.
a1.than: The conjunction than belongs to the formula comparative + than and does not fit after superlative.
a1.during: During my life does not combine with the one-time event the best day.

hints: One-syllable adjectives add -er; comparison with than.

a1.easier: Adjectives ending in -y change to -ier in comparative: easy → easier + than ...
a1.more easy: Short adjectives use the suffix -er instead of the construction more + adjective.
a1.the easiest: The formula the + adjective-est forms a superlative, not a comparison of two objects.
a1.far easier: The intensifier far can stand before comparative: far + comparative + than.

hints: Adverbs ending in -ly: comparative through more + adverb.

a1.more slowly: Adverbs are compared using the model more/less + adverb + than.
a1.slower: Slower is the form of the adjective slow; for the adverb, you need more slowly.
a1.slowliest: The superlative of the adverb is formed as the most slowly, not by adding -est.
a1.a lot more slowly: A lot / much can strengthen comparative: a lot more slowly than ...

hints: Near → nearer/farther for ordinary distance comparison.

a1.nearer: The one-syllable near forms the comparative by the rule adjective + -er + than: near → nearer.
a1.more near: For short adjectives, more + adjective is not applied.
a1.nearest: The nearest — superlative for selection from a group, not for comparing two houses.
a1.far nearer: Far can strengthen the degree: far + comparative + than.

hints: In the construction not as ... as, the base form of the adjective is used.

a1.difficult: The template not as + adjective + as uses the base form without suffixes or more/most.
a1.more difficult: Comparative + than is not included in the construction not as ... as.
a1.most difficult: Superlative (the most + adjective) does not combine with the scheme not as ... as.
a1.so difficult: So + adjective is usually continued by that + clause; in this construction, as ... as is needed.

hints: Superlative for distance: the farthest/the furthest (in travel, the farthest is most common).

a1.farthest: Superlative from far: the + farthest/furthest + noun, especially with ever + present perfect.
a1.farther: Farther — comparative form (farther than ...), not superlative.
a1.more far: Far has irregular degrees (farther/farthest or further/furthest), not the form with more.
a1.most far: The standard model of the superlative for far is farthest/furthest, not most far.

hints: Thin → thinner for ordinary comparison; much + comparative for strengthening.

a1.thinner: Thin → thinner is formed by doubling n and the suffix -er in comparative.
a1.more thin: Short adjectives are compared with the suffix -er, not the construction more + adjective.
a1.thinnest: The thinnest — superlative used for a group, not for comparing two states.
a1.a lot thinner: A lot serves as a booster before comparative: a lot + comparative + than.

hints: Irregular comparison good → better → the best.

a1.better: Irregular pair of degrees: good → better → the best; comparative is not formed with more.
a1.more good: Good does not combine with more for comparison, the form better is used.
a1.gooder: For good, the suffix -er is not added; there is only the irregular form better.
a1.much better: The amplifier much is located before irregular comparative: much better than ...

hints: Superlative for busy: the busiest. We double the consonant y → iest.

a1.busiest: Busy → busiest: y → iest in the superlative with the article the.
a1.more busy: More + adjective + than creates comparative, but the presence of the signals superlative.
a1.busyest: Spelling of the superlative: y changes to i before the suffix -est → busiest.
a1.most busy: The form most busy is less commonly used; the typical formula is — the busiest.

hints: Few → fewer (with countable nouns), fewest — superlative.

a1.fewer: With countable nouns, comparative fewer + plural noun is used.
a1.less: Less is typically used with uncountable; for the number of people, fewer is more correct.
a1.fewest: The fewest — superlative form, and in the sentence, comparative is needed.
a1.a lot fewer: A lot works as an amplifier in the model a lot + comparative.

hints: Superlative with the: the trickiest. Comparative: trickier.

a1.trickiest: The + adjective-est forms the superlative: the trickiest part.
a1.more tricky: More + adjective creates comparative, which does not combine with the article the in this sentence.
a1.trickier: Comparative (adjective + -er) is not used with the to describe one element from a group.
a1.most tricky: Most + adjective — alternative superlative, but the standard form for tricky is — trickiest.
